levels.description: Pod database-pod is stuck in ContainerCreating. Run kubectl get pvc -n k8smissions and check the STATUS column of the PVC it depends on.
levels.hint1:
ContainerCreating that never progresses usually means a Volume cannot be prepared. The Volume here depends on a PVC. Check the PVC status:

  kubectl get pvc app-storage-claim -n k8smissions
  kubectl describe pvc app-storage-claim -n k8smissions

What does the Events section say about why it cannot bind?
levels.hint2:
The PVC is Pending because no available PV satisfies it. PV binding requires three things to match: access mode, storage size (PV must be >= PVC request), and storageClassName.

Compare the PVC requirements with what the PV offers:

  kubectl get pv app-storage
  kubectl get pvc app-storage-claim -n k8smissions
levels.hint3:
The PV offers 1Gi with storageClassName: standard. The PVC requests 5Gi with storageClassName: fast. There are two mismatches — both the size and the storage class.

Fix both the PV's capacity and storageClassName in manifest.yaml to match what the PVC requires.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Two mismatches prevent binding:
1. PV capacity is 1Gi but PVC requests 5Gi — a PV must be at least as large as the PVC request
2. PV storageClassName is `standard` but PVC requires `fast` — the class must match exactly

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, update the PV:

```yaml
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 5Gi
  storageClassName: fast
```

## PV binding rules

Kubernetes binds a PVC to a PV only when ALL of these match:
- `accessModes` — both must include the same mode
- `storageClassName` — must be identical
- `capacity` — PV capacity must be >= PVC request

A mismatch in any one of these leaves the PVC in Pending forever.

levels.description: Pod web-app crashes on startup even though a volume is mounted. Run kubectl logs web-app -n k8smissions and read the error message.
levels.hint1:
The logs show 'ERROR: Config file not found at /app/config/app.conf'. The container is looking for the file at a specific path. A volume IS mounted — but is it mounted at the right location?

Check where the volume is actually mounted:

  kubectl get pod web-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'volumeMounts'
levels.hint2:
The volumeMount shows `mountPath: /data`. The container expects files at `/app/config`. The volume exists and is bound, but the mount point is wrong — the config files are written to `/data` but the app reads from `/app/config`.

Also check the init container's mountPath:

  kubectl get pod web-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A20 'initContainers'
levels.hint3:
The init container writes to `/data/app.conf` and the main container reads from `/app/config/app.conf`. Both are wrong paths — both need their mountPath changed to `/app/config`.

Find the volumeMounts section in both containers in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The init container mounts the volume at `/data` and writes the config file there. The main container also mounts at `/data` — but looks for the file at `/app/config/app.conf`. The file exists at `/data/app.conf`, not where the app expects it.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change mountPath in both the init container and the main container:

```yaml
volumeMount:
  name: config-volume
  mountPath: /app/config
```

Apply this to both the initContainer and the main container's volumeMounts.

## Why this matters

A volume is just a directory that Kubernetes mounts at a path you choose. If the mount path does not match what the application expects, the files are invisible to the app — even though they are right there on the same volume.

levels.description: A 3-replica Deployment web-servers needs all pods to share the same storage volume. Some pods are failing to mount the volume. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and look for volume-related errors.
levels.hint1:
Check the PVC's access mode and compare it with what a shared multi-pod scenario requires:

  kubectl get pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions
  kubectl describe pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions

What access mode is it using? What does that access mode allow?
levels.hint2:
The PVC uses `ReadWriteOnce`. RWO means the volume can be mounted by only ONE node at a time. In a multi-node cluster, if the second and third pod land on different nodes, they cannot mount the already-mounted volume.

Check the PV as well:

  kubectl get pv shared-storage -o yaml | grep 'accessModes'
levels.hint3:
Both the PV and PVC need to use `ReadWriteMany` — the access mode that allows multiple nodes to mount the same volume simultaneously.

You must change accessModes in BOTH the PV and PVC in manifest.yaml. A PVC cannot request an access mode that the PV does not support.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`ReadWriteOnce` (RWO) restricts the volume to a single node writer. With 3 replicas potentially spreading across nodes, pods on the second and third node are blocked from mounting the volume.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change both resources:

```yaml
# PersistentVolume
accessModes:
  - ReadWriteMany

# PersistentVolumeClaim
accessModes:
  - ReadWriteMany
```

## Access mode summary

- `ReadWriteOnce` (RWO) — one node mounts read-write (most common; fine for single-pod workloads)
- `ReadOnlyMany` (ROX) — many nodes mount read-only
- `ReadWriteMany` (RWX) — many nodes mount read-write (requires NFS, CephFS, or similar shared storage)

Note: hostPath volumes in kind support RWX only because it is a single-node cluster. In production, RWX requires network-attached storage.

levels.description: StatefulSet postgres-cluster has 3 replicas, all writing to what looks like separate storage. But data is being overwritten between pods. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and kubectl get pvc -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
In a healthy StatefulSet, each pod should have its OWN dedicated PVC. Check how many PVCs exist compared to the number of pods:

  kubectl get pvc -n k8smissions
  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=postgres

Are there 3 PVCs (one per pod) or just 1?
levels.hint2:
There is only one PVC — `database-storage` — shared by all 3 postgres pods. All pods are writing to the same location, which means each pod overwrites the other's data. This is never correct for a database cluster.

Check the StatefulSet spec to see how the volume is defined:

  kubectl get statefulset postgres-cluster -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A10 'volumes'
levels.hint3:
The StatefulSet references the shared PVC directly under `spec.template.spec.volumes`. This is wrong for StatefulSets.

StatefulSets have a dedicated field for per-pod storage: `spec.volumeClaimTemplates`. When used, Kubernetes automatically creates one PVC per pod named `<template-name>-<pod-name>`.

Replace the volumes section with a volumeClaimTemplates section in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

All 3 postgres pods mount the same PVC via `spec.volumes.persistentVolumeClaim`. Kubernetes allows this, but it means all pods share the same directory — disastrous for a database.

## Fix

Remove the `volumes` block and add `volumeClaimTemplates` to the StatefulSet spec:

```yaml
volumeClaimTemplates:
- metadata:
    name: database-storage
  spec:
    accessModes:
      - ReadWriteOnce
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 5Gi
    storageClassName: standard
```

With this, Kubernetes creates:
- `database-storage-postgres-cluster-0` for pod 0
- `database-storage-postgres-cluster-1` for pod 1
- `database-storage-postgres-cluster-2` for pod 2

Each pod gets isolated storage that survives pod restarts.

levels.description: Pod data-processor is stuck in ContainerCreating. Run kubectl get pvc -n k8smissions and check whether the PVC has bound to any storage.
levels.hint1:
The PVC is in Pending. For dynamic provisioning to work, the requested StorageClass must exist in the cluster — only then will a provisioner create a PV automatically.

List the available StorageClasses:

  kubectl get storageclass

Is `premium-ssd` in the list?
levels.hint2:
There is no StorageClass named `premium-ssd`. Without a matching StorageClass, no provisioner responds, no PV is created, and the PVC stays Pending forever.

See which StorageClasses actually exist:

  kubectl get storageclass

Note which one is marked `(default)`.
levels.hint3:
The cluster has a `standard` StorageClass available. The PVC in manifest.yaml requests `premium-ssd` which does not exist.

Find `spec.storageClassName` in the PVC in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`storageClassName: premium-ssd` — no StorageClass with this name exists. The dynamic provisioner only watches for PVCs that request a class it manages. Since no provisioner knows about `premium-ssd`, the PVC is permanently Pending.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the PVC:

```yaml
storageClassName: standard
```

## How dynamic provisioning works

1. PVC created with a storageClassName
2. The provisioner for that class detects the new PVC
3. Provisioner creates a matching PV and binds it
4. Pod can now mount the volume

If step 2 never happens (wrong class name), the chain stops at step 1.

levels.description: Pod web-app shows CreateContainerConfigError and will not start. Run kubectl describe pod web-app -n k8smissions and read the Events section.
levels.hint1:
CreateContainerConfigError means Kubernetes cannot build the container's environment before starting it. This happens when a referenced ConfigMap key does not exist.

Read the Events:

  kubectl describe pod web-app -n k8smissions

Which key is missing?
levels.hint2:
The Events say something like 'key database_host not found in configmap app-config'. Check what keys the ConfigMap actually has:

  kubectl get configmap app-config -n k8smissions -o yaml

Compare those keys with what the pod's env section references.
levels.hint3:
The ConfigMap has `app_name` and `app_version` but is missing `database_host`. The pod env references `database_host` via configMapKeyRef — and since the key is absent, the container cannot start.

You need to add the `database_host` key to the ConfigMap data in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod references `key: database_host` from ConfigMap `app-config`. That key does not exist in the ConfigMap's data section. Kubernetes cannot inject the environment variable and refuses to start the container.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add the missing key to the ConfigMap:

```yaml
data:
  app_name: "MyApp"
  app_version: "1.0.0"
  database_host: "postgres.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local"
```

## Tip: optional keys

If you want Kubernetes to skip a missing key instead of failing, add `optional: true` to the configMapKeyRef:

```yaml
configMapKeyRef:
  name: app-config
  key: database_host
  optional: true
```

The env var will be empty instead of causing CreateContainerConfigError. Use this only when the application handles a missing value gracefully.

levels.description: Pod database-client is running and connecting successfully. But a security audit has flagged the db-credentials Secret as a potential vulnerability. Run kubectl get secret db-credentials -n k8smissions -o yaml and read what you see.
levels.hint1:
The Secret data shows base64-encoded values. Many engineers assume this is a form of encryption. Test that assumption — decode one of the values right now:

  kubectl get secret db-credentials -n k8smissions -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

What do you see?
levels.hint2:
The decoded output is the plaintext password — readable by anyone with `kubectl get secret` access. Base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. Anyone with access to the namespace can decode every Secret trivially.

Check who currently has access to read Secrets:

  kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n k8smissions --as=system:serviceaccount:k8smissions:default
levels.hint3:
The default ServiceAccount in k8smissions can read Secrets — meaning any pod in this namespace can read all Secrets. The risk is not in how the values are stored (base64), but in who is allowed to access the Secret object.

The fix for this level is demonstrating you understand the risk: use `stringData` instead of `data` in the Secret so you do not have to manually base64-encode values, and note that real protection requires RBAC restrictions or an external secrets manager.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Nothing is broken — the pod works. The issue is a common misconception: base64 is NOT encryption. The Secret value `c2VjcmV0cGFzczEyMw==` decodes instantly to `secretpass123`. Anyone with `kubectl get secret` access can read every Secret in the namespace.

## The safer way to write Secrets

Use `stringData` so you do not manually base64-encode values:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
type: Opaque
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: k8smissions
stringData:
  username: admin
  password: secretpass123
```

Kubernetes base64-encodes it for storage. The values are still readable by anyone with access.

## Real security requires

- RBAC that restricts which ServiceAccounts can `get` Secrets
- Encryption at rest (enabled at the cluster level via EncryptionConfiguration)
- External secrets managers (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager via External Secrets Operator)

levels.description: Pod writer-app crashes immediately after the volume mounts. Run kubectl logs writer-app -n k8smissions to read the error.
levels.hint1:
The logs print 'ERROR: Permission denied writing to /data'. The container is running as a non-root user and the mounted volume directory is owned by root.

Check what user the container runs as:

  kubectl get pod writer-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'securityContext'
levels.hint2:
The container runs as `runAsUser: 1000`. Mounted PVC volumes default to being owned by root (UID 0). User 1000 has no write permission on a root-owned directory.

There is no `fsGroup` set at the pod level. `fsGroup` is what tells Kubernetes to change the volume's group ownership before mounting it into any container.
levels.hint3:
The fix is adding `fsGroup: 1000` to the pod-level `securityContext` (not the container-level securityContext).

In manifest.yaml, add this at `spec.securityContext`:

```yaml
spec:
  securityContext:
    fsGroup: 1000
```
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container runs as user 1000. The PVC volume is mounted with ownership root:root (0:0). User 1000 has no write permission on a root-owned directory.

## Fix

Add `fsGroup` to the pod-level securityContext in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  securityContext:
    fsGroup: 1000   # pod-level
  containers:
  - name: app
    securityContext:
      runAsUser: 1000
      runAsGroup: 1000   # container-level
```

Kubernetes changes the volume's group ownership to `fsGroup` before mounting it. Since the container's runAsGroup is also 1000, it has write access.

## fsGroup vs runAsGroup

- `runAsGroup` — the primary group of the process inside the container
- `fsGroup` — the group ownership applied to mounted volumes (set once at pod startup)

These are on different levels: `fsGroup` is pod-level (applies to all volumes), `runAsGroup` is container-level (affects only that container's process).

levels.description: A PVC was accidentally deleted and all the data it held is now gone. Run kubectl get pv -n k8smissions and look at the STATUS column of PV important-data.
levels.hint1:
The PV status shows Released or it may have already been deleted. When a PV's reclaimPolicy is Delete, deleting the bound PVC triggers automatic deletion of the PV and its underlying storage.

Check the current policy:

  kubectl get pv important-data -o yaml | grep 'persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy'
levels.hint2:
The reclaimPolicy is `Delete`. This is why the data is gone — the moment the PVC was deleted, Kubernetes automatically deleted the PV and its backing storage too.

For a PV holding important production data, this is the wrong policy. Look at what `Retain` would do differently.
levels.hint3:
The PV's `persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy` field needs to be changed from `Delete` to `Retain`. With Retain, deleting the PVC releases the PV but does not destroy the data — a human must manually recover or clean up.

Find this field in the PV spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Delete` — when the PVC was deleted, Kubernetes automatically deleted the PV and its backing storage. The data is permanently gone.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the PV:

```yaml
spec:
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain
```

## Reclaim policy lifecycle with Retain

1. PVC deleted → PV status changes from `Bound` to `Released`
2. PV data is preserved on disk
3. A human administrator reviews, recovers, or manually deletes the PV
4. A new PVC can be manually bound to the released PV by setting `spec.claimRef`

## Rule of thumb

For any PV holding data you care about, always use `Retain`. `Delete` is convenient for ephemeral test environments but dangerous in production.

levels.description: Users report that uploaded files disappear every time the pod restarts. Run kubectl logs data-app -n k8smissions and look for the warning message about storage type.
levels.hint1:
The logs say 'WARNING: Using emptyDir - data will be LOST on pod restart!'. Confirm this is the volume type in use:

  kubectl get pod data-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'volumes'

What kind of volume is it?
levels.hint2:
The volume is `emptyDir: {}`. An emptyDir volume is created when the pod starts and destroyed when the pod is deleted or rescheduled — its lifecycle is tied to the pod, not to the data.

Verify by deleting and recreating the pod:

  kubectl delete pod data-app -n k8smissions

After it restarts, check if the file is still there.
levels.hint3:
You need to replace the emptyDir volume with a PersistentVolumeClaim. The PVC has its own lifecycle — independent of any pod — so data survives pod restarts, rescheduling, and even node changes.

In manifest.yaml:
1. Add a PVC definition above the Pod
2. Replace the emptyDir volume with a persistentVolumeClaim reference
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`emptyDir: {}` — this volume is created fresh every time the pod starts. Any file written to it is lost the moment the pod is deleted or rescheduled. For user uploads or any persistent data, emptyDir is the wrong choice.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add a PVC and change the volume:

```yaml
# Add before the Pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: app-data
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
# In the Pod spec, replace emptyDir:
volumes:
- name: data
  persistentVolumeClaim:
    claimName: app-data
```

## When emptyDir is correct

emptyDir is useful for: scratch space, caches, sharing files between containers in the same pod. Never use it for data that must survive pod restarts.

levels.description: PVC data-pvc has been in Pending for twenty minutes. No pod depends on it yet — it just will not provision. Run kubectl describe pvc data-pvc -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The describe output should show in Events why no provisioner has responded. Look for messages about the StorageClass.

Then list what StorageClasses are installed in this cluster:

  kubectl get storageclass

Is `fast-nvme` in the list?
levels.hint2:
There is no StorageClass named `fast-nvme`. Dynamic provisioning works like a request: the PVC announces what StorageClass it needs, and the provisioner for that class creates a matching PV. If the StorageClass does not exist, no provisioner sees the request.

Note which classes DO exist and which one is the default (marked with `(default)`).
levels.hint3:
The PVC in manifest.yaml requests `storageClassName: fast-nvme` which does not exist. Change it to a class that is actually installed — `standard` is available in this cluster.

Find `spec.storageClassName` in the PVC in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`storageClassName: fast-nvme` — this StorageClass does not exist. No provisioner is registered for it. The PVC waits indefinitely for a PV that will never come.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  storageClassName: standard
```

## How to find available StorageClasses

```bash
kubectl get storageclass
```

Look for `(default)` — that class is used when no storageClassName is specified. You can also omit `storageClassName` entirely to use the default, but being explicit is better for clarity.

## Difference from level-5

Level 5 had a pod blocked by the pending PVC. This level isolates the PVC problem alone — same root cause, but here you see that a PVC can be stuck even before any pod tries to use it.

levels.description: PV db-data-pv exists and is currently Available. A new team is about to bind a PVC to it, but a senior engineer noticed a dangerous setting before any data was written. Run kubectl get pv db-data-pv -o yaml.
levels.hint1:
Look at the `persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy` field in the PV spec:

  kubectl get pv db-data-pv -o yaml | grep 'persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy'

What happens to this PV when the PVC that binds to it is deleted?
levels.hint2:
The policy is `Delete`. This means if anyone runs `kubectl delete pvc` in the future, the PV and all its data will be automatically destroyed — no confirmation, no recovery.

For a PV that will hold database data, this is a ticking time bomb. The fix must happen NOW, before data is written.
levels.hint3:
Change the `persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy` from `Delete` to `Retain` in the PV spec in manifest.yaml.

Note: if the PV is already bound to a PVC, you cannot change reclaimPolicy via apply — you would need to patch it directly. Since this PV is still Available, the manifest change will work.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Delete` on a PV intended for database storage. If the bound PVC is ever deleted — accidentally or during a cleanup — the underlying data is gone immediately and permanently.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain
```

## The three reclaim policies

- `Retain` — PV keeps data after PVC deletion; admin must manually recover or clean up
- `Delete` — PV and backing storage are automatically deleted when PVC is deleted
- `Recycle` — deprecated; do not use

## This is different from level-9

Level 9 showed the aftermath — data already gone. This level is the prevention: catching the wrong policy before it causes data loss. In real teams, reviewing PV reclaim policies is part of production readiness checks.

levels.description: Pod config-reader is Running but crashes because it cannot find a config file. Run kubectl logs config-reader -n k8smissions and read which file is missing.
levels.hint1:
The logs show `cat: /config/logging.properties: No such file or directory`. The ConfigMap has this file — so why is it not in the container?

Check what files are actually present at the mount path:

  kubectl exec config-reader -n k8smissions -- ls /config/
levels.hint2:
Only `app.properties` is in `/config/`. The ConfigMap has both `app.properties` and `logging.properties` — but only one appears. This happens when a ConfigMap volume uses the `items` field to selectively project keys.

Read the volume definition:

  kubectl get pod config-reader -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A15 'volumes'
levels.hint3:
The `items` list under the configMap volume only includes `app.properties`. When `items` is used, ONLY the listed keys are projected into the container — everything else is excluded.

You need to add `logging.properties` to the items list in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ConfigMap volume uses `items` to select which keys become files. Only `app.properties` is listed — `logging.properties` is silently omitted. The container expects both files but finds only one.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add the missing entry to the volume's items list:

```yaml
volumes:
- name: config
  configMap:
    name: app-config
    items:
    - key: app.properties
      path: app.properties
    - key: logging.properties
      path: logging.properties
```

## items vs no items

- **With `items`** — only the listed keys are projected; unlisted keys are excluded
- **Without `items`** — all ConfigMap keys are projected as files

Use `items` when you want to rename a key to a different filename (e.g. key `app.conf.prod` → path `app.conf`). If you just want all keys as files, omit `items` entirely.

levels.description: Pod api-consumer reads its API token from a mounted Secret, but every API call fails with 'authentication error'. Run kubectl exec -it <pod> -n k8smissions -- cat /secrets/token and look at what value is actually there.
levels.hint1:
The value inside the container does not look like a valid API token — it looks like another base64 string. This is the classic sign of double-encoding.

Decode the Secret value manually to check:

  kubectl get secret api-secret -n k8smissions -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d

Does the output look like a real token, or like another encoded string?
levels.hint2:
The decoded output is another base64 string — meaning the Secret's `data.token` field contains base64(base64(realToken)) instead of base64(realToken).

When Kubernetes injects the value into the container, it decodes it once — leaving behind an inner base64 string instead of the actual token.

Decode twice to find the real token:

  kubectl get secret api-secret -n k8smissions -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d | base64 -d
levels.hint3:
The real token is `supersecret-api-token-12345`. In manifest.yaml, the `data.token` field currently holds the base64 of that real token encoded again. It needs to hold ONLY the single base64 encoding of the real token.

Replace the value in manifest.yaml with the correct single-encoded base64:
`c3VwZXJzZWNyZXQtYXBpLXRva2VuLTEyMzQ1`
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Someone ran `echo 'supersecret-api-token-12345' | base64 | base64` (encoding twice) and pasted the result into the Secret's `data` field. The Secret stores double-encoded base64. When injected into the container, Kubernetes decodes it once — leaving behind the inner base64 string, not the real token.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, replace the token value with a single base64 encoding:

```yaml
data:
  token: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXQtYXBpLXRva2VuLTEyMzQ1
```

Or use `stringData` to avoid manual encoding entirely:

```yaml
stringData:
  token: supersecret-api-token-12345
```

## Why stringData is safer

With `stringData`, you write the plain value and Kubernetes base64-encodes it for storage. No manual encoding, no double-encoding risk. The `data` field requires you to encode manually — one extra `| base64` in your pipeline corrupts the value.

levels.description: Multiple pods need to read from and write to a shared PVC simultaneously, but only one pod can mount it at a time. Others are stuck waiting. Run kubectl get pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Check the PVC's access mode:

  kubectl get pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'accessModes'

What does `ReadWriteOnce` actually mean? Does it allow multiple pods on different nodes to mount the same volume?
levels.hint2:
`ReadWriteOnce` means only one node can have the volume mounted read-write at a time. In a multi-node cluster, the second pod on a different node cannot mount an RWO volume that is already mounted elsewhere.

For shared access across multiple pods, you need `ReadWriteMany`.

Note: you cannot edit the accessMode of an existing bound PVC — the PVC must be deleted and recreated with the new mode.
levels.hint3:
Change `ReadWriteOnce` to `ReadWriteMany` in the PVC's accessModes in manifest.yaml.

Since you cannot update accessModes on a bound PVC, you will need to delete the existing PVC first:

  kubectl delete pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions

Then apply the updated manifest.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]` restricts the volume to a single node. When multiple pods on different nodes try to mount it simultaneously, only the first node wins. Subsequent pods on other nodes are blocked — they either fail to mount or wait indefinitely.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteMany
```

Delete and recreate the PVC (accessModes are immutable on bound PVCs):

```bash
kubectl delete pvc shared-pvc -n k8smissions
```

Then apply the updated manifest.

## Difference from level-3

Level 3 required changing both the PV AND PVC access modes, plus had a full Deployment scenario. This level isolates the PVC-only change and adds the important detail: you cannot mutate an existing bound PVC — delete and recreate is the only path.
